rules.rule: If a strawberry pattern is visible (red pseudonetwork around open follicular openings surrounded by white halos), it is Actinic Keratosis.
If rosette structures are present (white four-leaf clover-like or four-dot structures visible within follicular openings), it is Actinic Keratosis.
If dotted or glomerular vessels are arranged in a regular or cluster pattern over a pink/erythematous background, it favors Actinic Keratosis.
If the background color is pink or erythematous (red) without the brownish-yellow pigmentation typical of seborrheic keratosis, it favors Actinic Keratosis.
If surface scale or keratin deposits are visible as white or yellowish scaling over a pink base, it favors Actinic Keratosis.
If follicular openings are clearly visible and prominent without milia-like cysts filling them, it favors Actinic Keratosis.
If milia-like cysts are present (multiple white or yellowish round, well-defined dots scattered throughout), it is Benign Keratosis.
If comedo-like openings (dark, plugged pore-like structures) are visible, it is Benign Keratosis.
If a cerebriform (brain-like) pattern of gyri and sulci is present, it is Benign Keratosis.
If the lesion has a brownish-yellow or tan color with a 'stuck-on' warty texture and thick ridges, it is Benign Keratosis (seborrheic keratosis).
If hairpin vessels with white halos are present (U-shaped or looped vessels each surrounded by a white halo), it is Benign Keratosis.

If no strawberry pattern, no rosette structures, no follicular openings with white halos, and no dotted or coiled vessels on a pink background are visible, the absence of all AK-specific patterns argues for Benign Keratosis.

If no milia-like cysts, no comedo-like openings, no cerebriform pattern, and no fissures or ridges are visible, and the lesion appears flat with a pink or erythematous background, the absence of all SK-specific structures argues for Actinic Keratosis.

If the background color is brownish-yellow or tan rather than pink or erythematous, and the surface appears keratinized or warty, the coloring argues against Actinic Keratosis and in favor of Benign Keratosis.
If the lesion background is clearly pink or erythematous with a scaly or rough surface and no SK-specific structures are visible, the pink inflammatory background argues for Actinic Keratosis.

If regression structures or blue-gray peppering are visible but no follicular openings with halos are present and the surface is flat without scale, consider lichenoid keratosis (a benign keratosis variant) rather than actinic keratosis — regression-like structures in the absence of actinic markers may indicate a regressing seborrheic keratosis.

If an atypical or irregular pigment network is present WITHOUT a clearly erythematous (pink/red) background AND without AK-specific vascular structures (no strawberry pattern, no dotted vessels on pink base), this pigment network pattern can occur in atypical seborrheic keratosis — favor Benign Keratosis over Actinic Keratosis.

If the lesion background is NOT clearly pink or erythematous (i.e., background is tan, brown, gray, or skin-colored) AND no dotted or glomerular vessels on an erythematous base are visible, the absence of erythematous background argues against Actinic Keratosis and favors Benign Keratosis.

If the lesion is flat with predominantly brown or dark brown pigmentation and a homogeneous, structureless, or diffuse pattern without any AK-specific feature (no strawberry pattern, no rosettes, no follicular red halos, no dotted vessels), favor Benign Keratosis — this pattern is consistent with flat seborrheic keratosis or LPLK.

If the lesion shows regression structures (white scar-like areas or blue-gray peppering) together with brown pigmentation but lacks the follicular red pseudonetwork and erythematous halo characteristic of Actinic Keratosis, this pattern indicates lichenoid keratosis (LPLK) — a benign keratosis variant — rather than Actinic Keratosis.
